exploits.name: Matcha
exploits.review:
Matcha is an external cheat featuring a built-in menu that provides access to preset functions such as ESP, aimbot, no-clip, and other similar tools. It does not support standard Lua execution, as it uses a custom Lua environment that runs within the cheat rather than inside the game.

What we like

  • Has a decompiler
  • Has a code explorer
  • Hybrid - kernel mode and user mode
  • Rich in features

What could improve

  • The authentication system is somewhat unintuitive
  • Initial setup may require some extra time
  • Registration process is somewhat complex and not user-friendly

summaries.pro: Feature-rich: ESP, aimlock, noclip, and more

warning.description: voxlis.NET recommends reading more about this product before continuing with any purchases, so you know what you are getting.

exploits.name: Potassium
exploits.review:
Potassium is an executor for Windows.

The developers operate under fresh accounts, and their identities remain unknown. However, the quality of their product suggests this is not their first time creating similar tools. They may be using new aliases to avoid exposing their primary identities in the event of any issues or malicious activity, although the exact reason for their anonymity remains unclear.

What we like

  • Has multi-instance support
  • Has a decompiler
  • High sUNC score
  • Works decently

What could be better

  • Detection issues
  • Users have reported compatibility and stability issues

summaries.neutral: Unknown developers, frequent ban waves, and stability problems

warning.description: This executor has a history of performing exit scams. Please read more about this product before continuing with any purchases, so you know what you are getting.

exploits.name: Volt
exploits.review:
Exploit Performance

  • Volt operates almost seamlessly as of March 2025, with only a few notable limitations. It does not support launching the official Roblox client because it relies on its own custom-built client environment with Hyperion Emulation. As a result, third-party launchers such as Bloxstrap and Fishtrap are incompatible. Despite this restriction, regular gameplay is generally unaffected for most users.
  • The second concern involves activity tracking. Volt has stated that running multiple third-party tools simultaneously is not permitted, although the exact limitations have not been clearly defined. This appears to apply particularly to VPS (Virtual Private Server) users. A VPS is essentially a remote computer that you rent—similar in concept to cloud-based systems—but instead of streaming like a traditional cloud gaming service, you are paying (often around $50 per month, depending on hardware) for access to a dedicated remote machine. According to their Privacy Policy and Terms of Service, these monitoring practices are formally enforced. Some users consider the policies to be overly strict, as enforcement may extend to flagging systems that have an unusually high number of active programs running (such as many applications open in the taskbar). This has led to concerns about how broadly activity monitoring is applied.
  • Lua decompilers are available, though most reliable options are paid, such as Oracle for $10. In practice, you generally do not need a Decompiler unless you are a developer or someone who saves game instances and requires the scripts in a readable format. A decompiler converts compiled scripts—normally readable only by a computer—into code that humans can understand. The result is not an exact 1:1 replica of the source, but the functionality remains intact. Variable names, structure, and certain definitions may differ from how the script was originally written.

Background Information

  • Between February 12–14, 2024, the first posts appeared on Reddit and V3rm.net about a new executor called Krampus / RO-EXEC. It was regarded as the first legitimate executor to bypass Hyperion, and attention spread rapidly—usage surged, and videos circulated widely. However, on May 11, 2024, Krampus exit scammed. After detection, the team claimed in their Discord that they were investigating the trigger, then later announced that their Chinese developers had been arrested and that Hyperion 4.0 had been released, providing fake documents as “proof.” Some time later, their Discord server was sold to a Robux casino, and no meaningful updates have been heard from the source since.
  • On November 24, 2024, AWP.GG was released, this time with Hyperion emulation. However, within a week, the community discovered that Krampus managed it, as they left a digital footprint in their DNS—specifically, the same Cloudflare nameservers used by Krampus.gg. The cycle repeated: after issues arose, AWP.GG announced on June 26, 2025 via Discord that their developers had been raided again, after which they went silent.
  • On November 10, 2025, AWP.GG rebranded to Volt as a closed beta. The first 100 former AWP.GG users with active subscriptions were allowed to migrate to the working beta version of Volt. On December 5, 2025, the project went public, allowing anyone to purchase Volt. The team announced that ownership had changed, though it remains unclear what occurred behind the scenes. Shortly after, on December 7, 2025, Volt took down everything—their website, Discord, and related services. However, within 72 hours, Volt returned. While the cause was never clarified, it is currently operating without further known issues.
  • March 6, 2026 — 20:30 (GMT+2): The day Volt was detected during a ban wave that hit nearly every executor. In the past, Volt’s developers had a habit of exit-scamming whenever their software was detected. This time, however, they appear to still be operating.

Developers Background Information

  • The developers operate under fresh accounts, and their identities remain unknown. However, the quality of the product suggests this is not their first time developing similar tools. They likely use new aliases to avoid exposing their primary identities, most likely due to their history of exit scams.

Sources: reddit.com/r/robloxhackers & v3rm.net

summaries.pro: Hyperion emulation and an easy-to-use UI

exploits.name: Volcano
exploits.review:
Exploit Performance

  • Volcano offers multi-instance support, allowing you to run multiple accounts on a single PC. It also includes a decompiler for viewing client scripts in-game. However, considering its price, there are better alternatives available as of January 2026.

Background Information

  • Volcano, originally known as Electron, was created in December 2021. At the time, it wasn’t particularly notable and served mainly as an alternative used by a small subset of the community, especially since more popular options like KRNL were available.
  • In June 2023, Electron made a comeback with a UWP exploit. While they were not the first to adopt this method, they dominated the market—likely due to greater name recognition than competitors like Celery, which was relatively unknown. Their key system was easy to complete, but the key duration was inconsistent, lasting anywhere from an hour to a full day.
  • After UWP got a Byfron patch, some time later, Electron was planned for release on December 1st on the Windows client, but delays pushed it back. It launched successfully, then became inactive, returned, functioned temporarily, and disappeared again—though this period is not fully documented. After the second patch, communication largely stopped on their official Telegram, and their Discord was locked.
  • On April 22, 2024, they announced via Discord that Electron would return under a new name: Volcano, with compensation for former Electron users. It took time for Volcano to regain traction, but it has since grown into a solid and active user base.
  • On December 11th, 2025, Volcano ditched the key system and switched to a fully paid model since Byfron’s changes made updating tough, and tons of users were bypassing the keys, costing the owner a lot of potential money.

Developer Information

  • The main owner is @coisop, known for contributions to Electron and Volcano. In 2019 work began on RyoWrapper, later renamed RyoProto; in 2020 Neutron released as a paid LuaJIT wrapper; in 2021 Electron launched as a free LuaJIT wrapper, the same year Luau dropped; Electron eventually transitioned to Proto Conversion with a Lua 5.1 to Luau transpiler; after the Luau source release the project continued using Proto Conversion alongside direct Luau source integration; later development progressed into Hyperion, which remains in use today.

Sources: reddit.com/r/robloxhackers, voxlis.NET & The Volcano Creator

exploits.name: Aimmy
exploits.review:
What we like

  • Aimmy supports games other than Roblox, since it is universal
  • There is no key system
  • Aimmy is open-source
  • An AI is trained and then used for aiming, making it indistinguishable from a real player

Some notes

  • AI like this is resource-intensive. Aimmy requires a GTX 1050/equivalent, or newer
  • The AI model needs to be manually trained first

summaries.neutral: Requires AI training on powerful hardware
summaries.pro: Supports other games & no key system

exploits.name: Arceus X
exploits.review:
Released in 2019, ArceusX was one of the first Roblox executors for mobile.

In March 2022, @lxnny - the owner of Delta - reposted a community warning on WeAreDevs, exposing ArceusX for "ratting" users and scamming developers. Despite this, ArceusX remains widely used.

In April 2024, SPDMTeam launched Wave, a windows executor which combined ArceusX with CodeX and an improved UI.

Although ArceusX is somewhat trusted, it is still owned by the same team which was involved with the aforementioned issues with data trust.

ArceusX is currently owned by Tiahh and SPDMTeam. There is limited public information about the developers.

What we like

  • Has support for VNG (Roblox Vietnam)

What could improve

  • Failures in the sUNC test need to be addressed

exploits.name: Bunni
exploits.review:
Originally released under the name "Swift", the brand was later sold and the project was renamed to "Bunni". It was subsequently rebranded to "Visual" for a short period of time, before returning to the "Bunni" name. For more history, please consult the Swift and MacSploit cards on this site.

Peyton, the project's creator, is an experienced and respected developer in the exploit community. His track record includes continued leadership on Swift, while now also overseeing Bunni's direction.

Blinx, the UI designer behind both the original Swift interface and Bunni's current look, brings a clean and consistent visual identity across the projects.

Reveny and Viz, both software engineering majors and long-time collaborators of Peyton, contribute to the development of Bunni. Though not publicly known in the community, their technical expertise ensures that the project is dependable.

What we like

  • Has a decompiler
  • Achieved a very high sUNC score

What could improve

  • Stability issues have been reported, with the exploit causing lag on some devices.

Additional notes

Visual C++ Redistributable downloaded for both x64 and x86 in order to make the UI work.

exploits.name: Delta
exploits.review:
delta is free, edited due of confusion, more info still in works

they have paid and free, free has keysysstem

exploits.name: Hydrogen
exploits.review:
Hydrogen has been a long-standing executor for macOS and Android (the Android version has been discontinued). Originally developed by @xgladius, it was known for being an ultra-fast macOS executor. Later, development was taken over by @retguard, who eventually discontinued updates in May 2024 due to personal reasons. As of April 12, 2025, @xgladius has resumed development, with plans to continue its progress for the foreseeable future.

Hydrogen is created by @xgladius, a well-known exploit developer and reverse engineer. He is recognised for projects like luaudec and various other contributions to the exploit community.

What we like

  • Customisable, open-source themes
  • Support for Luarmor
  • Performance is comparable to a standard executor
  • Has a decompiler
  • High sUNC score

exploits.name: Isaeva
exploits.review:
Exploit Performance

  • Currently being tested by 5 people by voxlis.NET selected ppl very cool ;P pls wait, thx mr owner for providing keys

Background Information

  • Nihon was started by @Immune and several other developers in October 2020. In its early stages, it received little traction and remained a smaller alternative within the exploit community. At the time, larger executors such as KRNL, Fluxus, and Oxygen U dominated the space. Despite this, Nihon gained recognition for its polished interface and solid execution strength, powered by its own custom solution. A similar situation occurred with Electron, which later rebranded into Volcano and eventually became successful.
  • Following the introduction of Hyperion, Nihon was briefly released on the UWP (Microsoft Store) version of Roblox. On August 28, 2024, a global banwave targeted UWP executors. By September 2024, UWP Nihon was patched, alongside every other UWP-based executor. For a short period, downgrading the UWP client remained a workaround, but this method only functioned for a few weeks before becoming unusable.
  • In early 2024, development activity increased significantly, leading to an official release on October 24, 2024, where Nihon delivered its strongest and most refined performance period. However, on February 3, 2025, the team split following internal conflict. The owner stated that a former administrator and Sero had nuked the Discord server and banned users, while clarifying that Zenith was not involved and had returned the server vanity URL. No further public explanation was given.
  • After this incident, Nihon became largely inactive aside from smaller releases. On January 11, 2026, Nihon was officially discontinued. Shortly after, on January 13, 2026, the project rebranded to Isaeva, which was officially released on February 22, 2026.

Sources: reddit.com/r/robloxhackers, voxlis.NET & Isaeva Team

exploits.name: Macsploit
exploits.review:
Macsploit is an executor specifically designed for macOS. While its stability is generally acceptable, it has experienced significant periods of downtime.

Owned by Raptor, Macsploit was originally founded by @Peyton and @Nexus42. Raptor initially began as a Windows executor collection. @Peyton retired from the project in 2022 but later returned and reignited activity around Macsploit, following concerns about how the project was being handled.

In 2024, the source code for Macsploit was open-sourced by @Peyton - the creator - after @managedhosts was accused of engaging in shady behaviour, including alleged involvement with loggers and other suspicious practices. Such allegations were found not to be true.

What we like

  • Has multi-instance support
  • Has a decompiler
  • High sUNC score

exploits.name: Serotonin
exploits.review:
Serotonin is an external cheat that has quickly gained a strong reputation from a development standpoint. It offers many unique features, including a 3D ESP Preview - a *first* for external cheats on Roblox - which is fully modular, allowing all elements to be adjusted. Other notable features include a Player Avatar/Mesh preview to visualize meshes and players - something never done before - and Highlight/Mesh chams that are comparable to the results of an internal.

Serotonin also provides a globally functional visibility check and lighting modules that work in almost all games. The development team maintains a consistent weekly update schedule.

Serotonin was launched by developer @Auti, who branched off from a previous private project called Melatonin and has approximately six years of experience in reverse engineering across numerous games. During a year and a half at Melatonin, Auti developed cheats for Roblox, FiveM, Battlefield, CS:S, Rust DMA, and Apex DMA. While there, Auti was responsible for releasing the first ever external visibility check to the public and enabling access to Instance Attributes through the external's Lua VM.

When starting Serotonin, Auti partnered with his friend @Fope, who is the UI creator and designer for the menu. Fope plans on releasing his CS:2 product and eventually expanding onto other games.

Auti occasionally posts Devlogs to his YouTube channel.

What we like

  • Now runs in kernel mode
  • Has a Lua environment/code explorer
  • Rich in features

exploits.name: Severe
exploits.review:
Severe gained recognition as the first undetected external following the rollout of Hyperion in November 2023. Since then, it has undergone continuous improvements, focusing on stability, performance, and a user-friendly interface with clean, modern visuals.

The custom Luau environment built into Severe is optimised for speed and deep memory interaction, offering advanced low-level capabilities rarely seen in external executors.

Severe originated as v-severe, an external that began development before Hyperion was introduced, sometime around 2021. it now includes unique features such as a 3D radar and in-menu chat - with additional enhancements planned for future releases.

Severe is developed by @BACKWOODS, who started his journey creating and redesigning UIs in C#. He later transitioned into exploit development, releasing Sona and Sona One, two prior executors known for working at levels 6/7 (at the time).

What we like

  • Has a decompiler
  • Has a code explorer
  • Rich in features

What could improve

  • A password reset costs $7.50...

exploits.name: Solara
exploits.review:
Solara offers solid performance with minimal crashes or instability, making it a reliable option for most users. It receives fast and frequent updates, keeping up with Roblox patches to reduce downtime.

Unlike many executors that rely on complicated key systems, Solara simplifies access by requiring users to complete a quick Linkvertise task during download, making setup more user-friendly.

Solara is owned and managed by @quivings, who was previously active in the script development scene. While not much is publicly known about him, he has built a respectable reputation within the community for his work and consistent updates to the tool.

What we like

  • Has multi-instance support
  • Has a decompiler

What could improve

  • Very low sUNC score

exploits.name: Vega X
exploits.review:
What we like

  • 2-day long keys

What could improve

  • The failure of sUNC checks

summaries.neutral: Fails sUNC, uses Arceus X module

exploits.name: Velocity
exploits.review:
What we like

  • Has multi-instance support
  • Has a decompiler
  • High sUNC score

What could improve

  • Stability issues

warning.description: The Wave team has split up and is riddled with major internal conflicts, so purchasing a key right now may be risky.

exploits.name: Wave
exploits.review: not finished, voxlis would recommend isaeva instead of this, or just get volt
